title: All Saints' Sunday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## November 3, 2019 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

LITURGICAL CONTEXT
All Saints' Sunday falls on the first Sunday following November 1st, making November 3, 2019 the observance date. The Revised Common Lectionary readings would have included Revelation 7:9-17 (the great multitude in white robes), 1 John 3:1-3, Matthew 5:1-12 (the Beatitudes), and Psalm 34:1-10. This falls in Ordinary Time, specifically the period after Pentecost and before Advent, creating a natural moment for the church to commemorate the communion of saints.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS
The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of General Convention 2018 (held in Austin, Texas in July 2018), which had addressed marriage equality and LGBTQ+ inclusion, with implementation of these resolutions ongoing in individual dioceses. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert J. O'Neill (who served 2008-2021), was working through these canonical changes. No major episcopal elections or controversies were dominating Episcopal news in early November 2019, though the Church remained focused on questions of inclusion and biblical interpretation that had animated the 2018 General Convention.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS
Denver experienced typical early November weather with possible snow or rain in the forecast after warmer October temperatures. The city was in the lead-up to the November 5, 2019 municipal election where Denver voters would decide on ballot measures including tax increases and affordable housing initiatives. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, home to Saint John's Cathedral, was undergoing continued gentrification with rising housing costs, a backdrop relevant to the cathedral's urban ministry. No major breaking local news dominated the immediate week before November 3.

NATIONAL NEWS
The Trump administration was engaged in the ongoing Ukraine telephone call controversy that would form the basis of the first impeachment inquiry; the House had announced the formal impeachment inquiry on September 24, and November 2019 was when depositions were beginning behind closed doors. The 2020 presidential campaign was intensifying with Democratic primaries drawing increasing media attention. The U.S. economy remained stable with low unemployment, though political divisions were deepening around trade policies and foreign affairs.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS
The Hong Kong protests, which began in June 2019 over the extradition bill, continued with escalating violence and the monthlong siege atmosphere persisting in November. The UK was in the runup to the December 12, 2019 general election called by Prime Minister Boris Johnson following Brexit stalemate. The war in Syria remained active with ongoing Turkish military operations in northern Syria following the U.S. withdrawal announcement in October.

CULTURAL MOMENT
American culture in early November 2019 was marked by deep political polarization surrounding impeachment proceedings, with cable news networks providing wall-to-wall coverage. The country was moving into holiday season preparation, with Christmas commercialism beginning in earnest. Denver's sports culture was focused on the Broncos' troubled 2019 NFL season (they would finish 7-9), a source of local frustration after years of playoff expectations following the 2015 Super Bowl win. A reflective sermon on saints and spiritual communion would have offered countercultural spiritual grounding amid the media-saturated partisan environment.
